title: Illustrate AI: Unveiling the World of Samplers
description:
lllustrate AI: Unveiling the World of Samplers

Dive deep into the vibrant world of AI-generated imagery with us! In this tutorial, we simplify the often intimidating terms you encounter in AI tools like "Samplers", "DDIM", and "DDPM".

🔍 What we cover in this video:

What are samplers and why do they matter?
A fun analogy comparing our AI model to a coffee machine. 
A quick look at the 10 models in Illustrate Ai.

🎨 Models discussed:

DDIM
DDPM
K_DPMPP_2M
K_DPMPP_2S_Ancestral
K_DPM_2
K_DPM_2_Ancestral
K_Euler
K_Euler_Ancestral
K_Heun
K_LMS

👍 Enjoyed the content? Do give us a thumbs up. Subscribe and hit the bell icon for more enlightening videos on AI and its magic. Your comments fuel our inspiration, so don't hesitate to leave your thoughts!

Chapters:
00:00 Illustrate Ai Samplers
01:33 Samplers Analogy
02:00 Samplers List
02:11 Samplers Test
03:08 Conclusion and Prompts

Stay creative and see you soon!

Join our community: https://www.facebook.com/groups/pixaromacommunity/

#illust
transcript: Welcome to another Illustrate AI tutorial.  Today, I'll be discussing samplers.  When you click on the Advanced Options button,  you'll find settings for the engine, steps, samplers, scale, and seed.  Within the engine settings, you can select your model.  For our purposes, the default model, which is the latest version of Illustrate AI, is the best choice.  You might not need to adjust this setting for now,  but as more models become available in the future, it might become relevant.  For now, let's dive into samplers and understand how they relate to the model.  When you click on Samplers, you'll notice terms like DDIM and DDPM.  But what do these samplers actually signify or represent?  To understand samplers, you first have to know what a model is.  In the world of machine learning an AI, a model is like a trained artist.  It's a system talk to do specific tasks, like recognizing photos,  making text, or for Illustrate AI, drawing pictures.  For example, Illustrate AI version 1.0 is one version of this artist or model.  This artist or model learns how to make art or pictures by looking at tons of other artworks.  This is its training data.  Everything the artist learns is stored in the model.  Now, every artist has their own way of making art, depending on the tools they pick.  For our model, these tools are called samplers.  They're like instructions telling the artist how to create a new piece.  In simple terms, samplers are the different techniques the model uses to make new data or images.  It's like choosing between painting with a brush or a palette knife.  Imagine a coffee machine analogy.  Think of a fancy coffee machine, our generative model.  This machine can produce a variety of coffees or images in our case.  The samplers are like the buttons or choices you press to get, espresso, cappuccino, latte, etc.  While the core mechanism, the coffee machine or the base model remains the same,  the method you use to get your final product, coffee or image, varies based on your choice.  Here are the 10 models in Illustrate AI as of this recording.  I'll show where each name comes from.  Feel free to pause the video to check them out.  I did some tests with a specific seed number and you'll see the outcomes can vary quite a bit.  You'll notice the samplers that made a different version than most are marked in red.  However, the results vary based on the prompt.  For some, the DDPM sampler made the change.  While for others, it was the Euler sampler.  Samplers make the image making a bit unpredictable.  So for some seeds, a tiny change can create a big difference in the outcome.  But for others, changes might be tiny.  Some seeds can be touchy and react a lot to small tweaks, giving very different pictures.  Meanwhile, other seeds are steadier and produce similar images no matter the sampler.  How the models been taught and the settings used can also affect how samplers work.  Some models might work better with certain samplers.  You can spot that the differences between samplers are minor,  like slight changes in the background.  While a few outputs vary, most of them look pretty similar.  To wrap up, samplers do their job quietly in the background.  You can experiment with them, but right now many give close to similar outcomes.  The default, auto, setting does a good job.  If you want more variety in your images, try tweaking the steps and scale settings.  Samplers play a key role in image creation.  Some samplers can make good images with fewer steps, while others might take longer but give more detail.  Some work faster, making them handy when you're using an image generator on your personal computer.  Depending on if you want quick, less detailed images or slower,  highly detailed ones, the number of steps becomes crucial.  If you found this video helpful or enjoyed the content, please give it a thumbs up.  Don't forget to hit that subscribe button and turn on the notifications so you won't miss out  on any future updates. Drop a comment down below with any thoughts or questions you might have.  Thanks so much for watching, and I'll see you in the next video.  Stay inspired.
